title: Split interaction modes per input source: separate Hold-to-select / Click-to-stay (Press for keyboard) for Mouse and Keyboard; always-on click-to-activate
description:
Make interaction modes (Hold to select / Click to stay open, from Bringr-93j.9) configurable separately for the Mouse and the Keyboard, rather than one shared global setting.

Why: I'd like different defaults per input source. For the mouse, "Hold to select" is much more convenient. For the keyboard, "Click to stay open" fits better. But I still want to be able to pick the other mode for each one if I prefer — so both modes must be selectable independently for both Mouse and Keyboard.

Renaming for the keyboard:
- For the Keyboard, rename "Click to stay open" to "Press" (you don't really "click" a keyboard).

Always-on click-to-activate:
- The "click an item to activate it and close the pie" behaviour (Bringr-93j.76) is always enabled — it doesn't need its own separate checkbox / option.
- Drop the separate toggle for that; clicking on an item always activates it and closes the pie.

Related to interaction modes (Bringr-93j.9), the click-to-activate option (Bringr-93j.76), and the Mouse + Keyboard activation reorganisation (Bringr-93j.69).
close_reason: Split interaction mode per input source (mouse default holdToSelect, keyboard default clickToStay shown as 'Press'); made click-to-activate always-on, removed its toggle. Controller modeProvider now takes a MenuTrigger; ClickToActivate enum and flag removed. 469 tests pass, swiftlint strict 0 violations.

title: Cleanups: simplify sorting (remove "Recently used") and remove the "Lock the cursor inside an app's windows" feature
description:
Two cleanups:

1) Simplify sorting:

Windows:
- The only available sort is "Fixed position". Remove any other window sort options.

Apps:
- Two sort options: Alphabetically, and by Dock position (Dock position is the default).
- Keep the "Keep Finder last" sub-option for the Dock-position sort (from Bringr-93j.55).
- Keep the "Don't sort my pinned apps" option (from Bringr-93j.43).

Completely remove the "Recently used" sort:
- Remove it for both apps and windows.
- Delete the implementation entirely (not comment out) — including any tracking, storage, or update code tied to recent-use ordering.
- This makes the broken-recently-used bug (Bringr-93j.85) moot.

2) Remove the "Lock the cursor inside an app's windows" feature:
- The optional cursor lock (Bringr-93j.29) that confines the pointer to the open app and its windows in the second-level pie should be removed entirely.
- Delete both the settings UI and the cursor-locking implementation.

Related to the sort-order preferences (Bringr-93j.34), the Dock-position sort with Keep Finder last (Bringr-93j.55), the don't-sort-pinned-apps checkbox (Bringr-93j.43), the broken-recently-used bug (Bringr-93j.85), and the cursor-lock feature (Bringr-93j.29).

title: All other windows blink for a fraction of a second when committing a selection
description:
When I select a window or app — either by releasing the mouse button or by clicking on the icon — at that moment all the other windows blink for a fraction of a second during the switch. It's a brief visual flash, but it's noticeable.

Ask: please look into what's causing the blink during commit and try to fix it.

Important — extremely careful change:
- Do NOT remove the previous implementation.
- Comment it out instead, and add comments explaining how the previous version worked, so it can be put back easily if needed.
- This change and debugging should be done really, really carefully — I'm worried that touching this commit path could totally break the activation / switch flow.

Low priority. Related to the select-and-commit behaviour (Bringr-93j.12).

title: "Recently used" sorting doesn't work for apps or windows (other sorts work fine)
description:
The "Recently used" sort option appears to be broken for both apps and windows — it doesn't actually order them by recent use.

Other sorts work fine:
- Apps: sort by name and sort by Dock position both work as expected.
- Windows: sort by fixed position works fine.

Only "Recently used" is broken (for both apps and windows).

Please debug this in the running app directly (logging, AX inspection, etc., as needed) to find out why the recent-use ordering isn't being applied.

Low priority. Related to the sort-order preferences (Bringr-93j.34) and the recent-use pollution bug (Bringr-93j.46).

title: Remove the now-unused window-parking reveal machinery (no strategy parks windows anymore)
description:
After Bringr-93j.83, no reveal strategy parks an app's windows off-screen at the window level (hide-others now just raises the hovered window, like raise-to-front). That left WindowController.hideOtherWindows + restoreCapturedFrame production-dead — only exercised directly by unit tests. Their only caller of system.park is gone, so park + WindowParkGeometry (LiveWindowSystem.park, FakeWindowSystem.park, the offScreenPoint sentinel, WindowParkPointTests) are also dead, as is the window-frame capture/restore plumbing built for parking: WindowSnapshot.originalPosition/originalSize, the RevealSnapshot position/size fields, and setPosition/setSize/position/size if nothing else needs them.

Deliberately deferred from .83 (a behaviour bead) because removal cascades through ~8 test files (offScreenPoint is woven into RadialNavigator{,Commit,Keyboard}Tests, RevealStateTests, WindowControlTests as in-flight-reveal setup scaffolding) and touches the US-015 crash-safety snapshot subsystem (RevealState persistence/restore-on-launch). Should be done as its own focused change with care, verifying the restore-on-launch safety net still round-trips without the position/size fields. Low priority: the dead code compiles, lints clean, and is still unit-tested, so it is harmless until removed.

title: Keyboard arrow keys don't work even when keyboard navigation is enabled
description:
The keyboard arrow keys have stopped working in the pie menu — they don't navigate at all, no matter whether keyboard navigation is enabled or not. Turning the keyboard navigation option on doesn't make the arrows respond.

Expected: with keyboard navigation enabled, the arrow keys should move focus as designed (Left / Right between items, Up / Down between layers).

Low priority. Related to the keyboard navigation work in Bringr-93j.71 and the follow-up Bringr-93j.72.

title: Remove (comment out) the "all Spaces" collection checkboxes; add a shared-Spaces note to the "all screens" option
description:
Two changes to the screen / space collection options (from Bringr-93j.48):

Remove the "all Spaces" checkboxes:
- Remove both "Include apps from all Spaces" and "Include windows from all Spaces" from the settings.
- Don't delete the implementation — just comment it out. It may be useful again in the future, but it isn't needed right now (and it's been the source of the phantom-window and off-Space collection issues).

Add a note to the "all screens" option:
- For the similar "all screens" option, add a notice / comment explaining that it only works when all displays share a single Space — i.e. when macOS's "Displays have separate Spaces" setting is turned OFF (System Settings -> Desktop & Dock, the Mission Control section). When that setting is ON (each display gets its own Space), the "all screens" collection won't work.

Related to Bringr-93j.48 and the all-Spaces issue in Bringr-93j.54.

title: Add a click-to-activate option (click an item to select it and close the pie), alongside release-to-select
description:
Right now selection happens only by releasing the activation shortcut — whatever is hovered (app or window) gets selected on release.

Add an option to also select by clicking: clicking on an item (an app or a window) activates that window / app and closes the pie menu.

So with this option enabled, the user can either release the shortcut to commit the hovered item as today, or click directly on an item to activate it and dismiss the menu.

Related to the interaction modes (Bringr-93j.9) and select-and-commit (Bringr-93j.12).

title: Appearance option to skip the second pie level when an app has zero or one window
description:
Add an appearance setting that disables the second pie level (the windows sub-wheel) for apps that have no windows or only a single window.

When this is on and you reach such an app:
- If the app has exactly one window, don't open a window sub-wheel — just act on the app directly (its single window).
- If the app has no windows, likewise don't open an empty second level.
- Only apps with two or more windows open the second-level wheel.

The point is to avoid a pointless extra level when there's nothing to choose between.

Belongs with the appearance settings (Bringr-93j.14); relates to the app-hover / window sub-wheel behaviour in Bringr-93j.10.

title: Keyboard nav optional checkboxes: auto-close on unsupported key, focus active window, auto-commit multi-window apps in numeric mode
description:
Optional keyboard-navigation checkboxes, related to Bringr-93j.71:

Close on unsupported key (new checkbox, default ON):
- Add a checkbox near the keyboard navigation settings. When checked (the default), pressing any key that isn't one of the supported navigation keys closes the pie menu.
- When unchecked, unsupported keys are ignored and the menu stays open.

Focus the active window when entering an app:
- When you open the menu and select / enter an application, the window that gets focused first (in the second level) should be that app's currently active window — not just the first one in the list.

Don't require a window choice for multi-window apps in numeric mode (new optional checkbox):
- In numeric mode, selecting a multi-window app currently drops into its windows and waits for a window choice; if you release the activation modifier (or press Escape) without choosing a window, the selection is abandoned and you revert to the previously active app.
- Example: holding the activation modifier (the user uses Fn+Option) to open the pie, pressing Ghostty's number activates it immediately and closes the pie because it has only one window. Pressing Google Chrome's number (it has two windows) previews Chrome and waits for a window choice; releasing the modifier or pressing Escape currently sends you back to Ghostty.
- With this checkbox on: releasing the modifier (or confirming) while on a multi-window app — without picking a specific window — should commit to that app instead (keep you on / activate Google Chrome and its active window), rather than reverting to the previously active app.

Related to Bringr-93j.71, Bringr-93j.72, and the focus / active-window behaviour in Bringr-93j.12.

title: Reimagine activation settings: replace Mouse+Trackpad with Mouse+Keyboard sections (remove duplicated modifier-key settings)
description:
Right now the activation settings have a Mouse section and a Trackpad section (from Bringr-93j.35), but the keyboard / modifier-key settings are literally duplicated between the two — the same settings appear in both, which we don't need.

Let's reorganize:
- Instead of separate Mouse and Trackpad sections, have a Mouse section and a single Keyboard (keyboard shortcut) section.
- The Keyboard section holds the modifier-key activation once, removing the duplication.
- The Trackpad section can be removed entirely — trackpad users summon the menu via the keyboard modifier anyway, so it doesn't need its own section.
- So we end up with just two sections: Mouse and Keyboard.

Add a small note / comment in the Keyboard section explaining that on a laptop without an external mouse, the keyboard is the only way to summon the menu.

Related to the activation work in Bringr-93j.35 and Bringr-93j.67. The modifier-key hold delay from Bringr-93j.58 would live under the Keyboard section too.

title: Allow mouse activation methods to be combined — modifier key and left+right click at the same time (not a radio)
description:
Right now the Mouse activation section (from Bringr-93j.35) treats its two methods — simultaneous left + right click, and modifier key — as a radio choice, so only one can be active at a time.

I'd like them to be independent instead, so I can:
- Use only the modifier key (left + right click disabled),
- Use only left + right click,
- Or have both enabled at the same time.

So change the mouse activation from an either/or radio into independent toggles, where either method — or both together — can be active.

title: Appearance: shadow opacity controls (glass block + separate text/icon shadow) and numeric fields for all sliders
description:
A few appearance enhancements:

- Glass shadow opacity: the liquid glass block has shadows — add a slider to control their opacity from 0% to 100%.

- Text & icon shadow opacity: add a separate control (its own slider) for the opacity of the text and icon shadows, independent of the glass block's shadow.

- Numeric input for all sliders: it would be great if every appearance slider could also have its value edited via a numeric field — so you can type an exact value instead of only dragging. This applies to all the sliders, not just the new ones.

Belongs with the appearance settings (Bringr-93j.14).

title: Add an Appearance checkbox to toggle the Liquid Glass effect on/off
description:
Add a checkbox in the Appearance settings that turns the Liquid Glass effect on and off.

When it's off, the pie menu uses the non-glass / fallback rendering instead. This is partly so we can easily test the older-macOS fallback (see Bringr-93j.64) without needing an older system — just flip the toggle — and partly to give users who prefer the plain look the option.

Belongs with the other appearance settings (Bringr-93j.14). Related to the Liquid Glass work in Bringr-93j.62 / .63 / .64.

title: Add an app ignore list — apps that should never appear in the pie
description:
Add an ignore list (exclusion list) of apps that should never be shown in the pie menu, no matter what.

Two user-friendly ways to add an app to the list:
- Pick an app directly from a list / picker of installed or running apps.
- Type entries manually as a comma-separated list — either bundle identifiers or application names.

Why: some apps linger in the Dock with no useful windows but still show up in the pie. For example, a Dell display utility stays stuck in the Dock even though it has no windows, and there's currently no way to keep it out of the menu. An ignore list lets the user permanently exclude apps like that.

Any app on the ignore list is filtered out of collection entirely, so it never appears in the wheel.

Name it whatever reads best — "Ignore list" or "Excluded apps". Related to app collection / filtering in Bringr-93j.48 and Bringr-93j.51.

title: Add a hold-delay slider/input for modifier-key activation (avoid accidental triggers, e.g. Fn language switch)
description:
Add a delay setting — a slider with a numeric input — that controls how long the activation modifier key(s) must be held before the pie menu opens. This applies to the modifier-key activation in both the Mouse and Trackpad settings (see Bringr-93j.35).

Why: some modifier keys do other things on a quick tap. For example, if Fn is set as the activation key, tapping Fn also switches the input language — and every time you switch language you don't want the pie to pop open. Requiring a short hold means a quick tap (to switch language) won't trigger the menu; only holding the key past the delay opens it.

Default: a small delay, e.g. around 100 ms.

Belongs with the activation settings in Bringr-93j.35.

title: Appearance setting to increase the pie's inner radius (distance from the point where it opens)
description:
Add an appearance setting that increases the distance between the point where the pie opens and the slices themselves — effectively a larger inner radius / padding, so the whole wheel sits further out from that center point (still centred on it).

Why it matters beyond looks: pushing the slices further out makes each slice's outer arc wider, so there's more room to land on a slice. That means the user doesn't have to be as precise when aiming, which improves usability — so this setting affects functionality, not just appearance.

Add it as an option that lets the user increase this distance / radius. Belongs with the other appearance settings (see Bringr-93j.14).

title: Add a "sort apps by Dock position" mode, with a "Keep Finder last" option
description:
A new app sort mode that orders the apps in the pie to match their position in the Dock — so the wheel mirrors your Dock order.

When this sort mode is selected, show one extra checkbox: "Keep Finder last".
- Finder is a special case: in the Dock it's always pinned to the very first slot and can't be moved or removed, so by Dock position it would always land first in the pie.
- With "Keep Finder last" on, force Finder to the end of the order instead of the front.
- With it off, Finder just takes its real Dock position — the first one.

This belongs alongside the other app sort options (see Bringr-93j.34). Low priority.

title: "Include windows from all Spaces" doesn't actually collect windows living on other Spaces
description:
The "Include windows from all Spaces" option isn't working.

Steps:
1. Open Google Chrome on the first Space.
2. Create a second Chrome window and move it to a new Space.
3. Open the pie menu.

What happens: Chrome still shows only one available window — the one on the current Space. The window living on the second Space isn't collected at all.

Expected: with "Include windows from all Spaces" on, Chrome should also show the window from the second Space. And when I activate that window, macOS should switch to the Space where that window lives.

Low priority. Related to the all-Spaces collection in Bringr-93j.48 and the phantom-windows bug Bringr-93j.52.

title: Extend the "hide everything else" checkbox to app selection, without minimizing windows
description:
Follow-up to Bringr-93j.27 (the "hide everything else after a selection" checkbox). Today that checkbox only does something when you select a specific window — it should also apply when you select an app. And the way it hides things needs to change: hiding all but one window of an app would mean minimizing the app's other windows, which is slow and unpleasant. Let's avoid minimizing altogether.

With the checkbox checked, there are two cases:

1. You select an app (not a specific window): hide all the OTHER applications' windows, and keep all of the selected app's windows on screen. Don't minimize anything — every window of the chosen app stays.

2. You select a specific window: same as above — hide all the other applications' windows and keep all of the selected app's windows on screen — but additionally activate the window you picked (bring it to the front / focus it).

The key point: never minimize the chosen app's other windows just to surface one. Hiding only ever applies to other apps; within the selected app, all windows stay visible and the chosen one (if any) is simply activated.
notes: Observed side effect: when an app's window has been hidden (not minimized — actually hidden, e.g. by this feature's "hide other apps" behaviour or via Hide / Cmd-H), that window is not collected the next time you open the pie menu, so it disappears from the switcher. The fix for surfacing those again lives in the collection settings — see Bringr-93j.50, which adds a "collect hidden windows" checkbox.

title: Recent-use sort gets polluted by previewing — hovering a window changes the Cmd+Tab order
description:
When the sort order is set to "by recent use" (for apps or for windows), just previewing a window in the menu changes the system's recent-use order (the Cmd+Tab order). Previewing brings the window forward to show it, and macOS treats that as actually using the window, so it jumps to the front of the recent list.

The side effect: the very next time I open the pie menu, the order is unpredictable — simply hovering over something I didn't actually want to pick has already reshuffled the recent-use ordering. So just browsing the menu pollutes the order it's based on.

What I'd like: previewing / hovering a window should not change the system's recent-use (Cmd+Tab) order. Only an actual committed selection should count as "using" a window. Please come up with a way to bypass or work around this so that hovering for a preview leaves the recent-use order untouched.

Low priority.

title: Show the Dock icon only while the Preferences window is open
description:
Right now Bringr lives only in the menu bar, so when you open Preferences the window appears but the app has no Dock icon at all — which feels a bit off for a normal window.

Let's make the Dock icon appear while the Preferences window is open, and disappear again once it's closed:
- Preferences open → the app shows a Dock icon and behaves like a normal windowed app.
- Preferences closed → no Dock icon, back to being menu-bar-only exactly as it is today.

Low priority.

title: Preferences for app and window sort order (default apps to Cmd+Tab order; windows by recent-use or static)
description:
I'd like to add a couple more preferences, both about sorting.

1. App sort order. A setting that controls how the apps are arranged in the pie. You can keep whatever sorting we do today as one of the options, but the default should match the Cmd+Tab order — the exact same sequence, laid out starting from twelve o'clock (the top) and going around the wheel.

2. Window sort order. A second setting that controls how an app's windows are arranged in the second-level pie. Two options:
- By recent use — windows ordered by how recently they were used.
- Static / fixed position — each window keeps a stable spot: the window that was open first stays first, the second stays second, and so on, so their positions don't jump around.

Important: keep this simple, don't over-engineer it. I don't want us writing or listening to events to track when windows open or how recently they're used and remembering their positions ourselves. These orderings should come straight from what macOS already gives us (e.g. the Cmd+Tab ordering and the system's own window order). If macOS doesn't actually expose what's needed for one of these options, don't build elaborate tracking to fake it — just update this task to note that it wasn't possible to implement, and leave it at that.

title: Update the README to reflect the app's current features and behavior
description: Bring the README up to date with where Bringr is now — its current features, how it works, and any recent changes — so it accurately reflects the app instead of being out of date.

title: Multi-monitor: only list apps and windows for the screen the menu was opened on
description:
With more than one monitor connected, the pie menu should only show the apps and windows that live on the screen where it was summoned — the currently selected screen — rather than everything across all the screens.

So if I open the menu on one monitor, I should only see the apps and windows that are on that monitor; anything sitting on my other screens shouldn't appear in the list.

title: Optional cursor lock that keeps the pointer on the open app and its windows in the second-level pie
description:
As a user, I want an optional setting that locks my cursor inside the second-level pie once it opens, so that I can't accidentally slide the pointer off the app and its windows while I'm choosing one.

This is an optional setting (off unless I turn it on). When it's on:

- The moment I hover into the second level of the pie (an app's window sub-wheel), the cursor becomes locked.
- While locked, I can only move the cursor among that app's window arcs (the second-level options) and the arc of the app that opened them. I can't move the pointer anywhere else on screen.
- When I move the cursor back onto the app arc I started from (the app that opened this second level), the lock releases and I can move around freely again — for example, back out to the other apps in the first-level pie.

When the setting is off, the cursor moves freely as it does today.

title: Preferences checkbox: hide everything else on screen after a selection is committed
description:
As a user, I want an option that clears everything else off the screen once I've picked something, so that I'm left with only what I selected and an uncluttered screen.

This is a checkbox in Preferences. When it's turned on:

- If I select an app, all the other apps on screen get hidden, leaving only the app I chose.
- If I select a specific window, all the other windows on screen get hidden, leaving only that one window.

When I release the mouse while hovering on an app itself — not drilling into one of its specific windows, just the app — and this checkbox is enabled, then by default all the other windows should be hidden as well, so committing at the app level also clears everything else away.

When the checkbox is off, selecting something behaves as it does today and nothing extra is hidden.

title: Refine the Preferences window and make it scrollable so everything fits
description:
The Preferences window is getting cramped — there's no longer enough room to comfortably show everything that's in it.

Refine the Preferences window and make its content scrollable, so that as more settings get added everything stays reachable instead of being cut off or squeezed into too little space.

title: "Hide others" mode feels slow at the window level because it minimizes other windows — find a faster way
description:
In "Hide others" mode, hovering over an app works well. But once you start hovering over the individual windows of a specific app, the mode reacts by minimizing all the other apps' windows to get them out of the way.

The problem is that minimizing windows in macOS is animated and quite slow, so doing it for everything makes the whole experience feel sluggish.

We should come up with a different way to get the other windows out of the way at the window level, without actually minimizing them. If genuinely hiding them turns out not to be possible, one idea is to simply move those windows instead — e.g. shift their position off to the side or out of view — so the result looks the same but happens instantly.

title: Three-finger click should suppress other trackpad gestures while it's used to open the menu
description:
Rework the MacBook trackpad behaviour so that when the menu is summoned with a three-finger click, no other trackpad gestures are allowed to fire at the same time.

The concern: many Mac users have Mission Control set to a three-finger gesture, so we want to make sure that using three fingers to open Bringr never accidentally triggers anything else.

In practice this probably won't clash, because Mission Control reacts to a three-finger swipe — it does not require an actual physical trackpad press or click, which is what Bringr relies on. So a real three-finger press shouldn't set Mission Control off.

Even so, while a three-finger click is being used to bring up Bringr's menu, the app should hold back any other gesture the system might otherwise read from those three fingers, so nothing else happens behind the menu.

I'm not sure whether this is actually possible — please look into it first, and if it is, implement it.

title: Three-finger gesture fires on tap/rest instead of requiring an actual click
description:
The three-finger trackpad activation (US-008, Bringr-93j.8) summons the menu as soon as three fingers land/rest on the trackpad — a light tap or even resting three fingers triggers it. It should fire only when the user performs an actual physical click (press) with three fingers, so accidental three-finger touches do not summon the wheel.

## Expected
Summon only on a real three-finger click (the trackpad physically clicks / force-click), not on a three-finger tap or rest.

## Actual
Any frame where exactly three fingers are present triggers a press.

## Suspected cause
ThreeFingerPressDetector.handle(fingerCount:) (Bringr/ThreeFingerActivation.swift:41) returns .press purely on the rising edge to requiredFingerCount fingers. It consumes only the finger COUNT (numTouches) from the MultitouchSupport contact callback (lines 81-89), which reports touch presence — not click or pressure state. There is no button/click gating, so a three-finger rest/tap is indistinguishable from a three-finger click.

## Likely fix
Gate the press on an actual click: require a physical trackpad click (leftMouseDown via NSEvent, or force-click pressure) while exactly three fingers are present, rather than firing on finger landing alone. Preserve the existing release semantics that hold-to-select uses to commit (US-009).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Three-finger activation fires only on a physical three-finger click, not on a tap or rest.
- [ ] A light three-finger tap or resting three fingers never summons the menu.
- [ ] The release signal for hold-to-select still works after a real three-finger click.
- [ ] One- and two-finger gestures still pass through untouched.
- [ ] Verified by build & run on a trackpad.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Switching interaction-mode setting kills hover feedback in the pie menu
description:
After changing the interaction-mode / mouse setting in Preferences ("When summoned:" picker, Bringr/PreferencesView.swift:36 — hold-to-select vs click-to-stay / mouse-release behaviour, US-009 Bringr-93j.9), summoning the pie menu and moving the cursor over slices produces no hover reaction: slices do not highlight and app/window isolation (US-010 / US-011) never triggers.

## Repro
1. Open Preferences and switch the interaction mode to the click/release-based mode (click-to-stay).
2. Summon the pie menu.
3. Move the cursor over apps/windows.
Expected: the hovered slice emphasises and the app/window isolates.
Actual: nothing happens — hover never updates.

## Suspected cause
Hover is driven solely by a global event monitor: RadialMenuWindow.startHoverTracking() installs NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (Bringr/RadialMenuWindow.swift:201), whose callback calls updateHover (line 213). The code comment (lines 66-67) assumes this "works in both interaction modes," but a global monitor does NOT fire for events delivered to your own application. In the click-based mode the overlay/app receives the mouse-moved events itself, so the global monitor never fires and updateHover is never called. In hold-to-select the originating app stays active during the held chord, so the events are global and the monitor works — which is why the break only appears after switching the setting.

## Likely fix
Also install a local monitor (NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents) and/or a tracking area / accept mouse-moved on the overlay window, so hover updates regardless of which app owns the events. Verify the overlay's activation/key behaviour in click-to-stay vs hold-to-select.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hover highlighting and app/window isolation work in BOTH interaction modes (hold-to-select and click-to-stay).
- [ ] Switching the interaction-mode setting and re-summoning never loses hover feedback.
- [ ] A test or harness exercises hover updates under both modes (or the monitor wiring is covered) so the regression is caught.
- [ ] Verified by build & run in each mode.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Release & distribution infrastructure (Bringr)
description:
Umbrella epic for shipping Bringr to users: tag-driven CI/CD, in-app auto-updates, and a public documentation website. Modeled on the setup in ~/Projects/ClipSlop.

Children:
- GitHub Actions: build + tag-driven release (and docs/appcast deploy).
- Sparkle: in-app automatic updates (key pair, Info.plist keys, appcast).
- Docusaurus: documentation website deployed to GitHub Pages.

The GitHub Actions child depends on both the Sparkle child (signs the DMG, updates appcast) and the Docusaurus child (docs-deploy workflow needs the site).

title: Initialize Sparkle for automatic updates in Bringr
description:
Integrate Sparkle 2 auto-updates the way ClipSlop does, so Bringr can update itself outside the App Store. This is the in-app + signing-key half; the release workflow (Bringr-zkc) consumes it to sign DMGs and publish the appcast.

HOW CLIPSLOP DOES IT:
- Sparkle SPM package (github.com/sparkle-project/Sparkle, from 2.6.0).
- A SparkleUpdater service wraps SPUStandardUpdaterController(startingUpdater: true), bails out unless Info.plist has SUFeedURL, and exposes checkForUpdates(); a Check for Updates menu item and an About-window button call it.
- Info.plist keys: SUFeedURL = https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mekedron/clipslop/main/appcast.xml and SUPublicEDKey = the Ed25519 public key.
- An Ed25519 key pair is generated with Sparkle generate_keys; the public key goes in Info.plist, the private key is stored as the GitHub secret SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY.
- On each tagged release, the workflow runs sign_update on the DMG and commits an updated appcast.xml to main.

ADAPTATION FOR BRINGR:
- Add Sparkle via Xcode SPM (Bringr is an .xcodeproj, not Package.swift). Check entitlements/sandbox implications for a status-bar app.
- Generate a NEW Bringr-specific Ed25519 key pair. Put SUPublicEDKey in Bringr Info.plist; set SUFeedURL to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mekedron/Bringr/main/appcast.xml; store the private key as the SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY GitHub secret; commit an initial appcast.xml at repo root.
- Add a SparkleUpdater service and a Check for Updates item to Bringr menu-bar menu / About window.

DEPENDENCY: the GitHub Actions release workflow (Bringr-zkc) signs the DMG and updates appcast.xml, so it depends on this task (key pair, Info.plist keys, initial appcast must exist first).
acceptance_criteria: Sparkle package added; SparkleUpdater wired with a working Check for Updates action; Ed25519 key pair generated; SUPublicEDKey + SUFeedURL set in Info.plist; SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY stored as a GitHub secret; initial appcast.xml committed.

title: Add Launch at Login option to Bringr
description:
Let users have Bringr start automatically at login, the way ClipSlop does. ClipSlop uses the LaunchAtLogin-Modern SPM package (github.com/sindresorhus/LaunchAtLogin-Modern, from 1.0.0), which wraps SMAppService (no legacy login-item helper bundle). It is exposed in the UI simply as LaunchAtLogin.Toggle(...) inside GeneralSettingsView, and listed as a credit in the About view.

ADAPTATION FOR BRINGR:
- Bringr is an Xcode project (not a Package.swift SPM project), so add the package via Xcode > File > Add Package Dependencies using URL https://github.com/sindresorhus/LaunchAtLogin-Modern and link it to the Bringr target.
- Bringr is status-bar-only (LSUIElement) with About and Preferences windows; add a Launch at login toggle to the Preferences window (or the menu-bar menu).
- Verify SMAppService registration works for a non-sandboxed status-bar app and persists across logout/reboot.
acceptance_criteria: A Launch at login toggle exists in Bringr Preferences; enabling it registers Bringr as a login item via SMAppService and it relaunches after logout/reboot; disabling it unregisters.

title: Build Docusaurus documentation website for Bringr
description:
Bringr has a docs/ folder but no published documentation website. ClipSlop ships a Docusaurus site under docs-site/ that is built and deployed to GitHub Pages by its deploy-docs.yml / test-docs-build.yml workflows. To reuse those workflows for Bringr (see the GitHub Actions port task), Bringr needs an equivalent Docusaurus site first.

SCOPE:
- Scaffold a Docusaurus site under docs-site/ (Node 20, npm), mirroring the ClipSlop layout at ~/Projects/ClipSlop/docs-site for reference.
- Wire existing Bringr docs/ content into the site and configure it for GitHub Pages (correct baseUrl/url for the mekedron/Bringr repo).
- Ensure npm ci and npm run build succeed locally so the CI workflows can build and deploy it.

This task unblocks the docs-deploy and docs-test workflows in the GitHub Actions port.
acceptance_criteria: A docs-site/ Docusaurus project exists; npm ci and npm run build succeed; site is configured for GitHub Pages deployment for the mekedron/Bringr repository.

title: Port ClipSlop GitHub Actions (CI + tag-driven release) to Bringr
description:
Replicate the GitHub Actions setup from ~/Projects/ClipSlop for Bringr. ClipSlop has three workflows under .github/workflows: (1) release.yml, (2) deploy-docs.yml, (3) test-docs-build.yml. Bringr has no .github yet. Remote already exists at git@github.com:mekedron/Bringr.git.

HOW CLIPSLOP WORKS:
- release.yml: triggered on push of a v*.*.* tag. Sets up Xcode (maxim-lobanov/setup-xcode), builds a universal binary, assembles a .app bundle, sets version from the tag, ad-hoc codesigns, builds a UDZO DMG, auto-generates release notes by grouping conventional-commit subjects (feat/fix/other) since the previous tag, then publishes a GitHub Release via softprops/action-gh-release. It also signs the DMG for Sparkle, commits an updated appcast.xml to main, and updates a Homebrew cask in a separate tap repo.
- deploy-docs.yml: on push to main touching docs, builds the Docusaurus docs-site and deploys to GitHub Pages.
- test-docs-build.yml: on PRs touching docs, builds the docs-site without deploying.

KEY ADAPTATIONS FOR BRINGR:
- ClipSlop is a Swift Package Manager project (swift build --arch arm64 --arch x86_64 + manual .app assembly). Bringr is an XCODE project: build with xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr (archive + export, or a Release build), which already emits a .app bundle, so the manual bundle-assembly steps differ.
- Bringr is a status-bar-only app (LSUIElement); keep that Info.plist key.
- Sparkle auto-update (appcast.xml signing) and the Homebrew cask update are OPTIONAL: Bringr has no Sparkle integration, no appcast.xml, and no Homebrew tap yet. Either omit these steps initially or set them up first (requires SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY and HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN secrets).
- The two docs workflows REQUIRE a Docusaurus docs-site, which Bringr does not have yet (see the blocking docs-website task).
acceptance_criteria: Three workflows live under Bringr/.github/workflows, adapted to the Xcode build. Pushing a v*.*.* tag produces an ad-hoc-signed .app and DMG attached to an auto-generated GitHub Release. The docs workflows build and deploy the Docusaurus site to GitHub Pages.

title: US-013: Reveal-strategy setting
description:
As a user, I want to choose how "other" windows/apps get out of the way so that the behavior matches my preference.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Preferences offers three reveal strategies: raise-target-to-front, hide-others, and dim-others.
- [ ] The chosen strategy applies to both the app-hover (US-010) and window-hover (US-011) levels.
- [ ] All three strategies are fully implemented and selectable.
- [ ] The setting is persisted and takes effect on the next summon without relaunch.
- [ ] A sensible default is set and documented.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Truncate labels that don't fit in their slice (ellipsis / text-overflow equivalent)
description:
Adjust text rendering for the pie's labels — right now text doesn't fit into the space it has and overflows / looks bad.

What I'd like: text that's too long for its slice should be truncated gracefully with a trailing ellipsis (the macOS / SwiftUI equivalent of CSS "text-overflow: ellipsis"), so labels always fit cleanly within their slice instead of overflowing.

This may be challenging given the arc geometry, but it should make labels read much cleaner.

Super low priority. Related to the appearance settings (Bringr-93j.14) and the overlay / radial layout (Bringr-93j.6).

title: PieSwitcher's own Preferences window doesn't appear in the pie menu (the app can't see itself)
description:
When PieSwitcher's Preferences window is open — and it has a Dock icon at that point (Bringr-93j.45) — the Preferences window never shows up in the pie menu. So the app can't see its own window.

Look into why PieSwitcher excludes itself from the collection (it's probably filtering out its own process / app), and fix it so that when its Preferences window is open it appears in the pie like any other app's window.

Super low priority — likely an easy fix.

Related to the Preferences Dock icon (Bringr-93j.45) and Dock-app collection (Bringr-93j.51).

title: Previewed windows aren't restored to their original stacking order after selecting a different window
description:
A very minor cosmetic bug. When previewing apps/windows, hovering brings a window up to the front so you can see it. But when you then select a different window, the windows you previewed along the way aren't put back to their previous place in the stacking order — they stay raised instead of returning to where they were.

The important part already works correctly: the window/app you actually choose ends up on top of everything else. This is only about the other windows you happened to hover over not being returned to their original order behind it.

Not important — super low priority — but if there's an easy way to restore the previewed windows to their original positions afterwards, it'd be a nice touch.
notes: Don't over-engineer this — no crazy hacks. If restoring the original stacking order turns out to carry any real risk (e.g. it could disturb the chosen window ending up on top, or otherwise make things worse), then leave the current behaviour intact and don't force a fix.

title: Optional trackpad haptic feedback when hovering pie items (trackpad-only)
description:
A nice-to-have feel feature: optional haptic feedback through the MacBook's trackpad. When there's no external mouse connected — so you're on the built-in trackpad — the trackpad's haptic engine gives a little tactile tap as you hover from one item to the next in the pie menu, so you can actually feel the selection moving.

Add a checkbox in Preferences to turn this on or off, and maybe a slider to adjust the strength/intensity of the haptic.

This naturally belongs with the Trackpad settings, and only applies when using the trackpad (no external mouse connected) — with a mouse there's no haptic engine to drive, so it simply doesn't apply.

Super low priority — purely polish.
